hints.usque: 'usque' is an adverb meaning 'all the way / as far as'
hints.Bethlehem: 'Bethlehem' is a proper noun — the name of the town (indeclinable)
hints.transire: 'transire' is the infinitive form — 'Transeamus' is the conjugated verb here

steps.target: to cross over / to go across
choices: to cross over / to go across

hints.to send across: 'to send across' is 'transmittere' — 'Transeamus' comes from 'transire' (trans + ire)

hints.Indicative: Indicative would state a fact — 'Transeamus' is a hortatory subjunctive (let us...)
hints.Imperative: Imperative gives a direct command — 'Transeamus' with -mus ending is 1st person plural subjunctive
hints.Infinitive: Infinitive would be 'transire' — 'Transeamus' is a conjugated subjunctive form

hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go) — 'transire' is its compound with trans-
hints.transmitto, transmittere, transmisi, tra: These are the parts of 'transmittere' (to send across)
hints.transfero, transferre, transtuli, transl: These are the parts of 'transferre' (to carry across)

hints.Jussive (let him...): Jussive uses 3rd person — 'Transeamus' is 1st person plural, expressing self-exhortation
hints.Purpose (in order to...): Purpose clauses are subordinate — 'Transeamus' is a main clause exhortation
hints.Result (so that...): Result clauses follow 'ut/ita' — 'Transeamus' is a main clause hortatory subjunctive

steps.target: all the way to / as far as
choices: all the way to / as far as

hints.until (temporal): 'usque' can mean 'until' in temporal contexts, but here with a place name it means 'as far as'
hints.everywhere: 'everywhere' is 'ubique' — 'usque' with a place name means 'all the way to'

translation: Let us cross over to Bethlehem
